segments.s234-g1: بایوان شد is a narrative past: a proper name followed by شد (past 'became/arrived') indicating the subject came onto the scene. نامه پاسخ نوشت is a typical Persian object-then-verb order: نامه پاسخ ('a reply-letter') is the object phrase and نوشت is the past-tense verb 'wrote'. The coordinating و links the two actions.
segments.s234-g2: بباغ بزرگی is an adverbial/prepositional phrase indicating location ('in/at a large garden'); بزرگی is an attributive noun meaning 'large/size'. درختی is an indefinite object 'a tree' (the -ی ending marks indefiniteness), and بکشت is an archaic past-form verb meaning 'he planted/caused to be (planted)', with the older prefix ب- preserved in classical style.
segments.s234-g3: نخست is an initial adverb 'first/first of all'. آفرین کرد is a verbal phrase meaning 'offered praise' (آفرین as 'acclamation/praise' with کرد as the past auxiliary/verb). بر is the preposition 'to/toward' that introduces the beneficiary/target, followed by کردگار 'the Creator/God'.
segments.s234-g4: کزو is a relative/pronominal adverb 'from whom / by whom' referring back to 'کردگار'; بود is the past copula 'was'; روشن‌دل و بختیار are predicative adjectives 'clear-hearted and fortunate' describing the subject whose wellbeing is ascribed to that Creator.
segments.s234-g5: This is a coordinated list of divine/celestial powers: خداوند ('the Lord/God'), ناهید (the goddess/planet Nâhid) and گردان سپهر ('the revolving/turning sky/heavens'), with گردان functioning as an adjective 'revolving' modifying سپهر.
segments.s234-g6: کزویست is a fused relative construction meaning 'from whom/which is' (indicating origin). It introduces a nominal list: پرخاش ('attack/anger'), آرام ('calm/peace'), و مهر ('and love/affection'); the nouns are conjoined without an explicit finite verb because the copula is incorporated in کزویست.
segments.s234-g7: سپهری is an adjective/noun 'celestial' or 'heavenly thing' functioning as the theme; برین گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in this manner / thus' (بر + این/ین + گونه 'way, manner'); بر پای کرد is a compound verbal idiom 'to set upon a base / to establish', where کرد is the past verb 'made/established'.
segments.s234-g8: شب و روز را is the definite object marked by را ('night and day' as the temporal frame), and گیتی آرای کرد is a causative/adornative phrase 'he adorned the world' (گیتی 'world' + آرای as the root 'adorn/ornament' plus کرد 'did'). The verb governs the whole object 'night and day' adverbially (i.e., he adorned the world continually).
segments.s234-g49: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase meaning “he said thus/so” (چنین = thus placed before the verb گفت = ‘said’). کز is the archaic contracted form of که از / از که and here introduces the reason/cause: «from/because of». جنگ افراسیاب is a genitive phrase ‘the war of Afrasiab’ (noun + name).
segments.s234-g50: This clause uses repeated negation around a verbless predicate: نه آرام باید نه خورد و نه خواب = ‘neither X nor Y nor Z’. باید here functions as an existential/obligational predicate (‘there ought to be / there is’ in poetic usage), while خورد and خواب are given as noun-like acts (eating, sleep). The structure omits an explicit auxiliary verb and lists the three things denied.
segments.s234-g51: مگر here introduces an exception/conditional (‘unless’). کان is an archaic/demonstrative particle (classical usage meaning roughly ‘that’ or ‘then’) and the phrase سر شهریار گزند is a compact nominal clause meaning ‘harm to the king’s head’ (سر شهریار = ‘the king’s head’; گزند = ‘harm’), with the copular/verbal element implied rather than stated.
segments.s234-g52: بخم is an archaic adjective modifying کمند (a snare or lasso); together بخم کمند = ‘a twisted/secret/cunning snare’. The sequence تو آید ببند combines a temporal/subordinate idea (‘when/if it comes’) with the imperative ببند (‘bind/close’). تو is the addressee (‘you’) and آید is third-person ‘it/that comes’, so the sense is ‘when it comes, you bind it’ (imperative + temporal clause).
segments.s234-g53: فریبرز is the subject (proper name). برگشت is the past verb ‘returned’. زان is the classical contraction of از + آن meaning ‘from that’, and بارگاه = ‘the palace/royal court’. Word order is subject–verb–adverbial phrase: ‘Fariburz returned from that court.’
segments.s234-g54: بکام دل شاه is a prepositional/possessive phrase meaning ‘to the pleasure/contentment of the king’s heart’ (بکام = ‘to the liking/pleasing of’, دل شاه = ‘the king’s heart’). ایران سپاه is a noun phrase ‘the army of Iran’ (ایران modifying سپاه). Together the phrase says the army of Iran returned in a way that pleased the king.
segments.s234-g17: همه آلت ناز و سورست و بزم is a compressed, poetic clause: همه آلت = 'all (my) means/devices'; ناز و (coquetry/caressing) pairs with سور/بزم (feast/assembly). سورست is the older/fused form (سور + است) meaning 'is a feast' or 'is revelry' — Persian poetry often fuses است to the preceding word. Word order is nominal (all + means + qualities) rather than a fully explicit clause with a separate copula.
segments.s234-g18: بپیش تو زین سان که آید برزم: بپیش ('in the presence of / before you') places the following phrase spatially/relationally; تو is the addressee. زین سان = 'in this manner' (here written as زین سان), where زین functions as a demonstrative 'this' + سان 'like/as'. که introduces a result or clause 'that ...'; آید is the present/jussive sense 'comes' and برزم is an archaic verb form (from رَزیدن) meaning 'I/it tremble(s) / shake(s)' or 'comes upon (me) with trembling' — so the clause says the situation before you is such that trembling comes upon (me).
segments.s234-g19: مگر آنکسی کش سرآید بپیش: مگر opens a negative-except/conditional clause 'unless'. آنکسی = 'that person / whoever.' کش here functions as an agentive/clitic element attached to the noun (classical Persian often uses -کش to mark an engager/one who exerts or carries out an action); سرآید is the present subjunctive/3rd-person form 'comes/appears' (سرآمدن = to come up/appear/raise one's head). بپیش places the action 'before' (i.e., 'before you' or 'to the front'). Overall: 'Unless some person comes forward (steps up) before [you]...'.
segments.s234-g20: بدین گونه سیر آید از جان خویش: بدین گونه is the fixed phrase 'in this manner' (here split in the word list as ب + دین, but in the text it functions as the single demonstrative 'بدین' + گونه 'manner'). سیر آید = 'becomes sated / comes satisfied' (سیر here is 'satiety' used predicatively, with آید 'comes' forming 'becomes sated'). از جان خویش literally 'from his life/his soul' — خویش (here represented as خوی + ش) is the reflexive/possessive 'his own'. The whole clause means 'he will thus depart sated from his life' (i.e., leave content with his life).
segments.s234-g21: وزان رنج بردن ز توران سپاه: وزان ('and from that / from that time') links this clause to prior events; رنج بردن is a verbal noun construction meaning 'to endure suffering' (here used nominally: 'the suffering endured'). ز introduces the source/agent 'from Turan' (ز توران) and سپاه 'the host/army' is the noun governed by ز. The phrase thus states that, as a result, there was the suffering of enduring Turan's army.
segments.s234-g22: شب و روز بودن به آوردگاه: شب و روز 'night and day' is an adverbial time phrase; بودن is the infinitive/gerund 'to be' used here as the predicate 'being' or simply 'were' (existence/continuous state). به آوردگاه = 'at/into the battlefield' (آوردگاه = battlefield). The clause says they were day and night in the battlefield (i.e., continually on the field).
segments.s234-g23: ز کارت خبر بد مرا روز و شب: ز کارت 'from your deed(s)/action(s)'; خبر بد 'bad news' (خبر modified by بد); مرا (represented as م + را) is the dative/accusative 'to me' or object-marked 'me' (here م is a contracted/clitic form). روز و شب 'day and night' is an adverbial time phrase attached to the whole object. Word order places the source and the news before the time phrase for emphasis.
segments.s234-g24: گشاده نکردم به بیگانه لب: گشاده 'open/frank' here functions as a participial/adjectival predicate 'to open (one's mouth)'; نکردم is the 1st-person past negative 'I did not do' (negated perfective). به بیگانه لب = 'to/for a stranger's lip' meaning 'to a stranger' (literally 'to a foreign lip' = speaking to a stranger). Together: 'I did not open my lips to a stranger' i.e. I did not speak to outsiders about it.
segments.s234-g9: یکی را چنین تیره‌بخت آفرید is structured as object (یکی را) + modifier (چنین تیره‌بخت) + verb (آفرید). یکی را: 'یکی' = 'one/someone' with را marking it as the definite/affected object; the subject (the maker) is omitted and understood. چنین modifies تیره‌بخت ('so / in this way ill‑fated'); تیره‌بخت is an adjectival compound used as a noun 'the ill‑fated one'. آفرید is simple past 3rd‑person singular 'made/created'.
segments.s234-g10: یکی را سزاوار تخت آفرید repeats the object phrase 'یکی را' (another person) + predicate 'سزاوار تخت' + past verb آفرید. سزاوار is an adjective 'deserving/fit (for)' that takes تخت ('throne') as the thing he is fit for; word order places the adjective before its complement. آفرید again is past 3rd‑person singular; the agent is still omitted/understood.
segments.s234-g11: غم و شادمانی ز یزدان‌شناس is a noun phrase 'sorrow and rejoicing' (غم و شادمانی) followed by the poetic preposition ز 'from' and یزدان‌شناس. یزدان‌شناس is a compound meaning 'the one who knows/knows God' (an epithet used for a pious/theological figure or, in context, the divine agent). The phrase says these experiences originate 'from' that figure.
segments.s234-g12: کزویست هر گونه بر ما سپاس is elliptical/poetic: کزویست = کز وی است 'which is from him'; هر گونه = 'whatever sort / whichever form'; بر ما سپاس literally 'upon us (is) thanks' i.e. 'we give thanks' or 'thanks are due from us'. Word order keeps the relative/demonstrative idea first then states that, whatever its kind, it brings gratitude from us to him.
segments.s234-g13: رسید آنچ دادی بدین بارگاه is formed with the verb رسید 'arrived/reached' fronted, followed by the relative phrase آنچ دادی 'that which you gave'. بدین بارگاه = 'to/at this court' (بدین = به + این 'to this'; بارگاه = royal court). Together: 'That which you gave reached this court.' Note: the agent of giving is 'you' (دادی), embedded in the relative phrase.
segments.s234-g14: اسیران و پیلان و تخت و کلاه is a simple enumerative list joined by و: 'captives (اسیران), elephants (پیلان), throne(s) (تخت) and hats/helmets (کلاه)'. Each و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. The nouns are direct items delivered to the court.
segments.s234-g15: هیونان بسیار و افگندنی places the plural noun هیونان 'beasts / animals' with بسیار 'many' following it (bestiary/quantity), then و and افگندنی, an adjectival/gerundial form from افگندن 'to cast' meaning 'to be cast / to be thrown/put'. افگندنی here describes a class of items (things/animals 'to be cast' or disposed of). The suffix -نی turns the verb into a deverbal adjective/noun.
segments.s234-g16: ز پوشیدنی هم ز گستردنی uses the poetic preposition ز 'of/from' twice in parallel: ز پوشیدنی = 'of the wearable/garments' (پوشیدنی as deverbal noun 'things to be worn' or 'wearables'), هم = 'also', ز گستردنی = 'and of the spreadable' (گستردنی = 'things to be spread' — e.g., carpets). The construction lists categories in parallel.
segments.s234-g41: ز + verbal noun: 'ز' here introduces the noun phrase built on the infinitive 'پوشیدن' and means roughly 'in/for the dressing (adornment) of'. 'شاه' is the genitive possessor ('of the king'). 'دستی' appears as the noun denoting a wrist/arm ornament (here an armlet/bracelet) and is followed by the adjective 'بزر' (archaic order: noun + adjective) meaning 'large/great'. The whole segment thus reads 'in dressing the king, a large armlet'.
segments.s234-g42: همان functions as a demonstrative ('that/the same'), pointing to the particular ornament just mentioned. The list 'یاره و طوق و زرین کمر' is a simple coordination: 'یاره' (a named ornament, here an armlet/bracelet — specialized meaning, not listed in the provided senses), 'طوق' = 'necklace/collar', and 'زرین کمر' = 'golden girdle' (زرین adjectival before/after can vary; here زرین+کمر = 'golden belt'). The conjunctions 'و' simply join the items.
segments.s234-g43: Word order places the recipient phrase with the object marker 'را' first: 'سران را' = 'to the chiefs/leaders'. 'همه هدیه‌ها' means 'all the gifts' or 'gifts for all', and 'ساختند' is the past tense 'they made/arranged'. Together: 'They made (arranged) gifts for the chiefs.'
segments.s234-g44: یکی introduces a single item ('one' / 'a certain'). 'گنج' = 'treasure'. The phrase 'زین سان' is an adverbial idiom meaning 'in this way / thus' (formed from زین + سان, with سان = 'like/as'); 'زین' here is an archaic element and is not the literal 'saddle' sense. 'بپرداختند' is past plural 'they paid out / they bestowed'. So: 'They bestowed one treasure in this manner.'
segments.s234-g45: This is a prepositional phrase identifying the person and accompanying items: 'فریبرز' is the name. 'با' = 'with' and the list 'تاج و گرز و درفش' names the regalia ('crown, mace, and banner'). The conjunction 'و' links the items in the usual way.
segments.s234-g46: یکی = 'one/a certain' introduces the gift. 'تخت' = 'throne' modified by 'زرین' = 'golden' (adjective after/before may vary in poetry), and 'زرینه کفش' = 'golden/gilded shoes' (زرینه an adjectival form agreeing with کفش). The segment lists the specific gifts given to Fariburz.
segments.s234-g47: Two verbs in sequence: 'فرستاد' = 'he sent' and 'فرمود' = 'he commanded/said'. 'تا' here introduces the intended/result clause 'so that / that' (not purely temporal), and 'بازگشت' is the verbal idea 'return' (here used as the action expected/ordered). So the sense is 'He sent and ordered (that they) return.'
segments.s234-g48: 'از' = 'from', marking the origin 'ایران' = 'Iran'. 'بسوی' = 'toward' introduces the destination 'سپهبد' (the spahbed / commander). 'گذشت' is the past of 'to pass/leave' = 'he passed/left'. The sequence thus says he departed from Iran toward the spahbed.
segments.s234-g25: شب و روز (lit. 'night and day') is a time adverbial meaning 'day and night'. بر پیش is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'before / in the presence of' and governs the following noun phrase یزدان پاک, an epithet 'the Pure God'. Word order is adverbial phrase + prepositional phrase + epithet; together they set the scene ('day and night before the Pure God').
segments.s234-g26: نوان بودم is a simple past copular clause: نوان 'youthful' + بودم 'I was'. و links the two predicates. دل‌شده is a compound participial adjective 'heart-made' = 'heart-struck / smitten', and the reduplicated چاک چاک intensifies the state as 'torn / rent to pieces' (reduplication is a poetic intensifier). The clause describes the speaker's condition.
segments.s234-g27: کسی را که introduces a relative clause 'the one who…' with را marking 'کسی' as a definite/ topical object. The clause that follows gives identity: رستم بود پهلوان = 'was Rostam, a champion' (classical/poetic word order places بود before the predicate پاہلوان at the end). Together this names 'the person who was Rostam the champion.'
segments.s234-g28: سزد‌گر here is an impersonal/formulaic word meaning roughly 'it is fitting' (literally 'one who judges fit'), followed by the present/subjunctive-form verb بماند 'that he remain'. همیشه جوان is the adverb + adjective 'always young' describing the desired state. The construction expresses a judgment/wish about the referent of the previous clause.
segments.s234-g29: This is an inverted classical order: the normal sense is 'سپهر ندارد پرستنده چون تو' — 'the heavens do not have a worshipper like you.' پرستنده 'worshipper/devotee' is compared with چون تو 'like you', and ندارد 'does not have' predicates the clause with سپهر 'the heavens' as the subject. The inversion is poetic emphasis on the heavenly lack.
segments.s234-g30: ز تو is a short poetic prepositional phrase 'from/about/for you' introducing whose fortune is meant; بخت 'fortune' is the topic. هرگز 'never' negates the wish; مبراد is an archaic/obscure jussive/negative-form verb meaning essentially 'may it not be taken away' (i.e. 'do not withdraw'); مهر 'favor/affection' is the object. Overall this is a protective wish: 'May fortune never take away its favor from you.'
segments.s234-g31: نویسنده 'the scribe/author' is the subject; پردخته is an adjectival past participle 'well‑wrought / finely finished'; شد 'became' gives a resultative/passive sense: 'the scribe became finely fashioned.' ز آفرین 'by praise' indicates the cause/instrument (آفرین = acclamation/praise rather than the verb 'create' here).
segments.s234-g32: This clause is transitive: نهاد is the past of نهادن 'he placed/put'. از بر is a poetical locative/phrasing meaning 'upon / on the surface of', followed by نامه 'book' with خسرو as its possessor ('Khosrow's book'). نگین 'a gem' is the direct object of نهاد. The clause therefore means 'he placed a gem upon Khosrow's book.'
segments.s234-g33: بفرمود is an honorific past-tense verb 'he commanded/said' (third-person). تا introduces a subordinate/result clause ('so that' / 'in order that'). خلعت is the direct object 'khalʿat' (robe of honour) and آراستند is a past‑plural verb 'they adorned/dressed' (the verb agrees with an unstated plural agent); together the clause means 'he commanded that they adorn the khalʿat.'
segments.s234-g34: This continues the command: 'ستام و کمرها' is a coordinated noun phrase (two items) and بپیراستند is past‑plural (بـ‑ as part of the verbal form) 'they adorned / they arrayed.' The verb agrees with the plural coordinate; ستام here is an archaic/poetic noun whose exact referent is unclear in isolation (see senses). کمرها is the plural 'girdles/belts'.
segments.s234-g35: صد introduces a counted partitive group; 'صد از جعد مویان' = 'a hundred of the curly‑haired' (از used partitivally). جعد 'curl' + مویان 'hairs' together produce the adjective 'curly‑haired'. زرین modifies کمر to give 'golden girdles' (کمر here = girdle). Word order in poetry can stack modifiers after the noun.
segments.s234-g36: صد اسپ = 'a hundred horses' (numeral + noun). گرانمایه is a post‑nominal adjective 'valuable/precious' modifying اسپ. با introduces a comitative/instrumental phrase 'with', and زین زر is 'saddle(s) of gold' (زین 'saddle', زر 'gold' used attributively).
segments.s234-g37: صد اشتر = 'a hundred ashter' (here 'اشتر' is the classical word for camel, though the provided senses don't list that sense). همه بار = 'all laden' (همه modifies بار 'load'); دیبای چین = 'folds (چین) of brocade (دیبا)' — چین here is the cloth 'fold', not the country. The phrase describes camels bearing brocade.
segments.s234-g38: The second clause repeats 'صد اشتر' ('a hundred camels'). ز before a verbal noun (ز افگندنی) introduces a purpose/participial sense: افگندنی is the verbal‑noun/infinitive of افکندن 'to place/put/load', so 'ز افگندنی' = 'for placing/loading'. هم‌چنین 'likewise' links this group to the previous list.
segments.s234-g39: ز here marks composition/material: 'of' or 'made of' (poetic usage). یاقوت رخشان = 'shining/radiant ruby' (adjective رخشان follows the gem). دو انگشتری = 'two rings' (numeral + noun). The whole = 'two rings of gleaming ruby.'
segments.s234-g40: ز again introduces materials/stones associated with the following noun: 'خوشاب و در' are listed materials/gems (خوشاب is a poetic gem‑name or 'pleasant‑water' metaphor for a gem; در here is most likely the classical poetic word for 'pearl' but that sense is not supplied in the list). افسری is 'a diadem/crest' and بر سری = 'upon the head'. So the phrase means 'a diadem upon the head set with khoshāb and pearls.'

sentences.s234-s1: Bayvān arrived and wrote a reply-letter, and in a large garden he planted a tree.
sentences.s234-s2: First he offered praise to the Creator, by whom he was clear-hearted and fortunate.
sentences.s234-s3: The Lord, Nâhid, and the revolving heavens—from whom come attack, calm, and love.
sentences.s234-s4: He set up a celestial sphere in this manner, and he adorned the world night and day.
sentences.s234-s25: He said thus: because of Afrasiab's war there is neither rest nor eating nor sleep.
sentences.s234-s26: Unless the king's head is harmed, (take) your twisted snare — when it comes, bind it.
sentences.s234-s27: Fariburz returned from that court, the Iranian army to the king's heart's content.
sentences.s234-s9: All my devices are for coquetry and feasting, and the entertainment before you is such that I tremble.
sentences.s234-s10: Unless someone steps forward before you, he will thus go away from his life sated.
sentences.s234-s11: And from that came the hardship of enduring Turan's host, being day and night on the battlefield.
sentences.s234-s12: From your deeds there came bad news to me day and night; I did not open my lips to a stranger.
sentences.s234-s5: He made one thus ill‑fated, and made another deserving of the throne.
sentences.s234-s6: Sorrow and rejoicing come from the knower of God; whatever form they take, we owe thanks to Him.
sentences.s234-s7: What you had given reached this court: captives, elephants, thrones, and helmets.
sentences.s234-s8: Many beasts — those to be cast and those to be worn, and likewise those to be spread.
sentences.s234-s21: In adorning the king they put on a large armlet — that yāreh — and a collar and a golden girdle.
sentences.s234-s22: They made gifts for the chiefs; they presented one treasure in this manner.
sentences.s234-s23: Fariburz, with crown and mace and banner, (was given) a golden throne and gilded shoes.
sentences.s234-s24: He sent (them) and commanded (it) so that (they) would return; then he departed from Iran toward the spahbed.
sentences.s234-s13: Day and night, in the presence of the Pure God, I was youthful, and my heart was torn to pieces.
sentences.s234-s14: As for someone who was Rostam the champion, it is fitting that he remain forever young.
sentences.s234-s15: The heavens have no worshipper like you; may fortune never withdraw its favor from you.
sentences.s234-s16: The scribe was finely fashioned by praise, and he set a jewel upon King Khosrow's book.
sentences.s234-s17: He ordered that the robe be adorned, and that the garments and girdles be made splendid.
sentences.s234-s18: A hundred of the curly‑haired with golden girdles; a hundred fine steeds with saddles of gold.
sentences.s234-s19: A hundred camels, all laden with folds of brocade; a hundred camels likewise for loading.
sentences.s234-s20: Two rings of gleaming ruby, and a diadem upon his head set with bright gems and pearls.
